title: FASD Respect Act
plain_summary: This bill helps prevent fetal alcohol spectrum disorders and supports people who have these conditions. Fetal alcohol spectrum disorders happen when babies are harmed by alcohol before they are born. The bill creates special centers across the country to teach people about these disorders, help find them early, and provide treatment. It also gives money to research programs and educational programs through 2029. The goal is to help families and make sure more people know how to prevent these disorders.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Advancing FASD Research, Services and Prevention Act or the FASD Respect Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Support for Individuals and Families Impacted by Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder
sections.text:
This section changes an existing law called the Public Health Service Act to better help people with fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD). FASD happens when a baby is exposed to alcohol before birth and can cause lifelong problems with thinking, behavior, and physical development.

The government will create or continue programs to help with FASD awareness, prevention, finding cases early, treatment, and services. These programs can include training health professionals at medical schools and other health training programs to better serve babies, children, and adults with FASD. They can also help agencies that provide health and mental health services.

The programs will support research on FASD and develop treatments and support systems that work well for different cultures and languages. This includes helping prevent alcohol use during pregnancy, which sometimes happens along with use of other drugs.

The government will work with medical experts to create better ways to diagnose FASD and will help states and tribal communities build their ability to identify, treat, and support people with FASD and their families. This might include training programs for professionals, creating awareness campaigns, and sharing information about available services.

To get funding, organizations must be a state, tribal group, local government, university, or nonprofit organization. They must submit an application explaining what they plan to do with the money. The government may require them to name a coordinator to oversee FASD activities and create an advisory committee to help make strategic plans.

FASD-informed support means using proven methods that consider cultural and language differences to improve the quality of life for people with FASD and their families.

The law also creates Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders Centers for Excellence. These centers will be run by organizations that know how to prevent, identify, and treat FASD. The centers will help build the ability of local, tribal, state, and national groups to prevent FASD and help people who have it.

These centers can expand diagnostic services, create public awareness campaigns about the risks of drinking during pregnancy, serve as information centers for best practices, provide training to various professionals including teachers and law enforcement, improve local support programs, and provide technical help to other funded programs.

The law authorizes Congress to provide the necessary money for these programs from fiscal year twenty twenty five through twenty twenty nine. Within four years of this law taking effect, the Department of Health and Human Services must report to Congress about their efforts to increase public awareness of FASD and identify the best ways to prevent, identify, treat, and support people with this condition.

Finally, the law removes an old section from the Public Health Service Act that is no longer needed.
